title: Cyberware & Augmentations
description: Implants, modifications, and augmentations found in a high-tech world — standard issue, black market, experimental, and the kind that come without a warranty or a removal service.

entries.result: A subdermal comm implant — standard corpo issue — that has been broadcasting its location to a server the owner never authorized.
A reflex booster, basic tier, that cuts reaction time by approximately 30%. Legal. Gives users headaches after about six hours of activation.
A synthetic eye replacement with standard visual augmentation — light amplification, zoom, recording. The recording function is disabled by the manufacturer and has been re-enabled by someone else.
A bone-laced knuckle reinforcement. Cheap, visible on an X-ray, and illegal to carry into most corpo facilities.
A neural skill chip for a specific technical discipline. The chip is read-only but someone has modified it to be writable.
A subdermal storage pocket — a sealed compartment under the skin, accessible via a release code. It contains something. The person who installed it is not here.
A military-grade targeting system, salvaged, that overlays weapon aim assistance into the user's visual field. The calibration is off for anyone other than the original user.
A pain inhibitor chip that blocks pain signals from reaching conscious processing. Effective. Increases the chance of unnoticed serious injury.
A language processor implant pre-loaded with six languages. The translations are slightly off in ways that matter in negotiation.
A subdermal power cell — a secondary battery providing emergency power to other implants when primary systems fail.
A biometric spoofer installed at the wrist that can mimic one stored identity's fingerprint, pulse, and body temperature signature.
A retinal display overlay that projects interface elements into the visual field. The UI skin is default corporate and can't be changed — the personalization module was disabled at purchase.
A micro-filament hand modification — near-invisible wires embedded in the fingertips that can pick electronic locks or manipulate fine mechanisms.
A black-market emotional dampener that reduces the neurochemical experience of fear and stress. Effective. Has begun reducing the user's experience of other emotions as well.
A corporate tracking implant, carrier-grade, designed to be undetectable. It is undetectable unless you know exactly what to scan for.
A voice modulator that can reproduce any recorded voice with fidelity. Requires a three-second sample minimum. Installed post-surgery, not pre-approved.
A synthetic muscle sheath on one arm that provides burst-strength enhancement but degrades after about two years and requires expensive replacement.
A toxin filter installed in the bloodstream that neutralizes common poisons and street drugs. Alcohol still works. Most corporate chemical agents do not.
An internal clock and recorder — timestamps everything the user experiences and stores up to ninety days of compressed sensory data.
A neural combat predictor that runs real-time probability on opponent movements. Accuracy decreases in environments with more than four combatants.
A skin-surface heat regulator that prevents hypothermia or hyperthermia across a wide temperature range. Not comfortable. Effective.
A deep-cover identity chip — full biometric package, different name, different history — installed beneath a false registration. The cover identity has a past.
A network interface jack installed at the base of the skull. Allows direct neural connection to compatible systems. The jack itself is standard. What it's been connected to is not.
A cardiac regulator that keeps the user's heart rate within a controlled range regardless of exertion or stress. Originally medical. Has been repurposed.
A memory editor — technically illegal in most jurisdictions — that can flag, suppress, or enhance specific memories at the user's command. Three memories are currently flagged.
A subdermal blade — a single mono-edged weapon that deploys from the forearm on command. Single blade. Fast deployment. The mechanism has started catching.
A neural firewall designed to resist intrusion by hostile netrunners. Corporate tier. Has been hacked once and the entry point was patched by a third party.
A low-output EMP emitter, palm-sized, that can disable electronics within two meters for about three seconds. Burns out after five uses.
A visual camouflage system — not true invisibility, but a light-bending surface film that makes the wearer extremely difficult to track in low-light environments.
A lung modification that allows the user to hold their breath for up to twelve minutes and filters toxins from polluted air.
A personality matrix overlay — an experimental system that allows the user to run a secondary behavioral profile over their own. The secondary profile has goals.
A signal interceptor installed behind the ear that passively captures local wireless traffic. Encrypted. The encryption is not the user's.
A reflex dampener — the opposite of a booster — used by operatives who need to appear unaugmented in situations where enhanced reaction time would be noticed.
A skill chip for combat — illegal to carry concealed in most jurisdictions, legal to own, gray-market to install. The combat style loaded is distinctive enough to be traced to a specific operator.
A synthetic corneal implant that records everything the user sees to an offsite server. The user agreed to this in a terms of service document.
A subdermal armor layer — not thick enough to stop serious rounds, enough to stop a stab wound. Also makes the user's skin feel wrong to the touch.
An olfactory enhancer that brings scent sensitivity to the level of trained animals. Useful. Makes cities significantly less pleasant.
A behavioral analysis system that reads micro-expressions and body language in real-time and overlays emotional probability data in the visual field.
A broadcast jammer implanted in the chest that produces a short-range privacy field. Disrupts all local wireless. Including the user's own systems.
A cortex bomb — a threat implant installed without the user's consent — designed to terminate the user remotely if they become a liability.
A hydraulic exo-joint kit for the legs that increases sustained running speed and jump height. Loud. Requires regular maintenance that the current owner is skipping.
A neural addiction suppressor that blocks the neurological reward loop associated with specific substances. It has been applied to suppress something other than drugs.
A full synthetic hand replacement with adjustable grip strength, built-in tools, and a lockpick mechanism in the ring finger.
A corporate loyalty chip — installed as a condition of employment — that monitors and logs employee sentiment, behavior flags, and physical location.
A blood oxygenator that allows the user to function at peak capacity with significantly less sleep than a baseline human requires.
A surveillance package — cameras mounted in the eyes, mics in the ears, all streaming to an external handler — designed for corporate intelligence gathering.
A coagulation accelerant that stops bleeding from wounds much faster than natural healing. Side effect: increased clotting risk in prolonged inactivity.
A deep cover comm system — undetectable to standard scanners — that uses bone conduction and subvocalizing to communicate.
A synthetic spine segment that has replaced two vertebrae, improving flexibility and durability but producing chronic low-grade interference on neural signals above and below.
A hormone regulation implant designed for extended undercover work that suppresses physical stress indicators — sweating, elevated pulse, pupil dilation — on command.
A hacking suite — a comprehensive netrunner package, medical grade — installed without documentation. The installer's signature is in the hardware.
A sensory package originally designed for industrial inspection work: acoustic analysis, material density sensing, trace chemical detection.
A neural throttle that limits the user's capacity for violence by blocking specific command signals at the motor cortex. It has been modified to be defeatable.
A subdermal radio that operates on a frequency used exclusively by a specific faction. The user may or may not know this.
A sleep suppressor that can eliminate the need for sleep for up to seventy-two hours. Comes with documented cognitive degradation warnings that the current user is ignoring.
A trauma stabilizer — a medical implant that kicks in when the user takes life-threatening damage and keeps them functional at a basic level for a short window.
A full limb replacement arm, military grade, that can toggle between human-normal performance and combat-specification performance. The toggle has a tell.
A memory chip containing a dead person's skills — a complete learned library, not personal memories — that can be accessed but not integrated cleanly with existing neural pathways.
A behavioral conditioning implant designed to produce loyalty and compliance in the user toward a specific individual or group. The recipient's consent is not documented.
A personal privacy shield — a signal jammer and sensor scrambler worn as a collar — that prevents the wearer from being tracked by most commercial surveillance.
A military-grade combat stimulant dispenser that injects directly into the bloodstream on a preset trigger. Crash after use is significant.
A visual overlay system used by corpo negotiators that highlights stress indicators, calculates optimal counter-arguments, and tracks body language in real-time.
A subdermal map display that projects navigational data as a faint image on the user's inner wrist. Standard issue, government sector.
A synthetic liver modification that processes toxins and drugs at an accelerated rate — useful for someone who needs to appear affected by substances without being affected.
An identity scrambler implant that loops false biometric data to corporate scanners. Has a 12% false positive rate that raises flags on high-security scans.
A retinal camera system that can photograph anything the user sees without any external tell — no shutter, no flash, no visible hardware.
A proximity alert system installed in the back of the skull that tracks movement within a ten-meter radius and alerts the user through a subtle haptic signal.
A skill suppressor — experimental, dangerous — that can temporarily mute a trained ability to make the user appear less capable than they are.
A crowdsourced tactical AI implant that improves in combat situations by connecting to a shared network of users in similar situations. The network is monitored.
A synthetic vocal cord modification that can produce a perfect copy of any voice with a four-second sample. Completely undetectable by hearing alone.
An outdated neural interface that is no longer supported by the manufacturer. Still functions. The unsupported firmware has been maintained by an anonymous third party.
A black-market endocrine hack that allows the user to manually trigger adrenaline, serotonin, or cortisol release at will.
A bone-mounted micro-storage device — the size of a grain of rice — surgically embedded in the user's sternum. Contains data. Not empty data.
A contact-patch stimulant delivery system — a single-use device embedded in the palm that delivers a compound through skin contact with a target.
A network isolation system that can completely sever the user from all data connections, including involuntary ones, in under a second.
A subdermal bio-tracker that monitors the user's location and health vitals. Originally parental issue. Still transmitting to an original registered device.
A full sensory recording suite — sight, sound, touch, smell — installed by a content company for immersive media production. The contract is still active.
A joint-mounted weapon system — a projectile weapon concealed within a shoulder modification — that has no visible tells until it fires.
A corporate-issue crowd management implant that was standard for law enforcement a decade ago, now discontinued. Still fully functional. Being used for something it wasn't designed for.
A neural killswitch — installed in someone else — that the user currently holds the activation code for. The arrangement was a deal. The deal may not still apply.
An emotion recorder that logs the user's emotional state continuously. The log has never been reviewed. It is very long.
A synthetic skin layer over visible augmentations that mimics baseline human skin so precisely that the underlying hardware is undetectable unless scanned or cut.
A micro-explosive charge concealed in a molar. A dead man's switch. The user set it themselves and may have forgotten about it.
A crowd behavior prediction system repurposed from mass event management into a personal threat assessment tool.
An experimental direct-feed stimulation system that produces sensory experiences — taste, touch, smell — without physical input. Currently set to a single recurring experience.
A hidden compartment in the forearm — not subdermal, mechanical — with a release that requires a specific grip pattern to open.
A full neural backup — a snapshot of the user's mind at a specific moment — stored on a drive that is not in their possession.
A spinal tap port that allows direct connection to compatible equipment for high-bandwidth data transfer. Medically gray-market.
An outdated military-issue language and behavior analysis system that was supposed to have been returned after service. It wasn't.
A black-market clone chip — a duplicate of someone else's complete neural identity package — that can be loaded over the user's own.
A threat-response implant that activates a hardened mode across all systems when the user's biometrics cross specific stress thresholds. The threshold has been set very low.
A corporate loyalty override — designed to prevent employees from taking certain actions against their employer — installed without the user's knowledge during a routine medical.
A muscle memory accelerator that allows physical skills to be practiced in neural simulation, compressing weeks of repetition into hours of experienced time.
A sleep learning system that loads skill chips during deep sleep cycles without conscious effort. The chips currently loaded are not the user's.
A comprehensive electronic warfare suite miniaturized for personal use — signal interception, jamming, and broadcast in a package the size of a large implant.
A behavioral recording implant installed by a court order as a condition of release. Still active. The review date passed eight months ago.
A full-body subdermal mesh that provides passive protection and sensory feedback. An earlier version with known failure modes. The failure modes haven't been patched.
An experimental consciousness fork — a system that allows parallel processing of two separate decision-making threads. The second fork has been active for six months and has developed a preference.

A black-box implant — sealed, unknown manufacturer, no documentation — that was already installed when the user woke up in a ripper doc's chair six months ago. It does something. They don't know what.

A full military-grade augmentation package, high-end, comprehensive — reflex, strength, neural, sensory — that should not exist in civilian hands, was not sold by the manufacturer, and does not appear in any procurement record.
